attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of painful fingers and toes that worsen in cold weather. She also reports fatigue, weight loss of 4 kg, and recurrent skin ulcers on her legs. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears unwell with vital signs within normal limits. There is purple discolouration of her fingers and toes with small ulcers on the tips. Several non-blanching purpuric lesions are present on her lower legs. Lymph node examination reveals generalised lymphadenopathy.

Initial investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 112 g/L (115-165)
  • White cell count 12.5 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)
  • Platelet count 145 x 10^9/L (150-400)
  • Serum protein electrophoresis shows a monoclonal peak in the gamma region
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features highly suggestive of type I cryoglobulinaemia, including cold-induced symptoms, skin manifestations, and evidence of an underlying lymphoproliferative disorder. The presence of a monoclonal gammopathy, lymphadenopathy, and cytopenias strongly supports this diagnosis.

Type I cryoglobulinaemia is characterised by the presence of monoclonal immunoglobulins that precipitate at cold temperatures. It is almost always associated with an underlying lymphoproliferative disorder such as multiple myeloma, Waldenström macroglobulinaemia, or other B-cell lymphomas. Clinical features typically include Raynaud phenomenon, acrocyanosis, and skin ulceration, particularly in cold-exposed areas.

Henoch-Schönlein purpura typically affects children and presents with palpable purpura, arthralgia, abdominal pain, and renal involvement. It is an IgA-mediated vasculitis and would not show cold-induced symptoms or monoclonal gammopathy.

Polyarteritis nodosa is a systemic vasculitis that can cause skin ulceration but does not typically show cold-induced symptoms. It usually presents with constitutional symptoms, mononeuritis multiplex, and renal involvement.

Systemic sclerosis can cause Raynaud phenomenon but is characterised by skin thickening and fibrosis, which are not present in this case. The monoclonal gammopathy would also be unusual.

Thromboangiitis obliterans typically affects young male smokers and causes inflammation and thrombosis in small and medium-sized arteries. While it can cause ulceration, the patient's age, gender, and presence of monoclonal gammopathy make this diagnosis unlikely.

The diagnosis of cryoglobulinaemia requires special laboratory testing where blood samples must be kept at 37°C during collection and transport to prevent premature precipitation of the cryoglobulins. Treatment involves managing the underlying lymphoproliferative disorder and protecting against cold exposure.
learningPoints: Type I cryoglobulinaemia is associated with underlying lymphoproliferative disorders and presents with cold-induced symptoms.
Raynaud phenomenon, acrocyanosis, and skin ulceration are characteristic features of cryoglobulinaemia.
Laboratory testing for cryoglobulins requires special handling with samples kept at 37°C during collection and transport.
references: Cacoub P, Vieira M, Saadoun D. Cryoglobulinemia - One Name for Two Diseases. N Engl J Med. 2024;391(15):1426-1439. doi:10.1056/NEJMra2400092
